Description: Use a connection pool to manage PostgreSQL connections efficiently. A Pool reuses idle connections, limits total connections, and handles automatic reconnection. Always configure max, idleTimeoutMillis, and connectionTimeoutMillis. Listen for pool-level error events to prevent unhandled crashes.
Do: Use Pool with explicit max connections and timeout settings; handle pool error events; call pool.end() on shutdown
Dont: Dont create a new Client per request; dont leave pool.max at default without benchmarking; dont ignore pool error events

Guideline: Parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection
Description: Always use parameterized queries ($1, $2, ...) to pass user input. The pg library separates query text from parameters at the protocol level, making SQL injection impossible. Never concatenate or template user input into query strings.
Do: Use $1 placeholders and pass values as the second argument array; validate input types before querying
Dont: Dont concatenate user input into SQL strings; dont use template literals to embed variables in queries

Description: Use explicit transactions when multiple statements must succeed or fail together. Acquire a client from the pool, issue BEGIN, run statements, then COMMIT. Always ROLLBACK in a catch block and release the client in a finally block to prevent connection leaks.
Do: Acquire a dedicated client for transactions; always ROLLBACK on error; always release the client in finally
Dont: Dont run transactions on the pool directly; dont forget to release the client; dont skip ROLLBACK on error

Description: Use a migration tool like node-pg-migrate or knex to version-control schema changes. Each migration should have an up and down function. Run migrations in CI/CD before deployment. Never modify production schemas manually.
Do: Use timestamped migration files with up/down functions; run migrations in CI/CD pipelines; test rollbacks
Dont: Dont alter production tables with raw SQL manually; dont skip down migrations; dont share a migration across unrelated changes

Description: Create indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses. Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify index usage. Prefer partial indexes for filtered queries and composite indexes for multi-column conditions. Drop unused indexes to reduce write overhead.
Do: Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify index usage; create composite indexes for multi-column WHERE clauses; use partial indexes
Dont: Dont create indexes on every column blindly; dont forget to analyze query plans; dont leave unused indexes

Description: Use JSONB columns for semi-structured or variable-schema data. JSONB supports indexing with GIN indexes, enabling fast containment and key-existence queries. Validate JSONB input with Zod or JSON Schema before insertion. Avoid using JSONB for highly relational data.
Do: Use JSONB with GIN indexes for flexible data; validate JSON structure before insertion; use containment operators
Dont: Dont store highly relational data in JSONB; dont skip validation on JSONB input; dont query JSONB without indexes on large tables

Description: Use environment variables for connection strings. Enable SSL in production with rejectUnauthorized set appropriately. Parse DATABASE_URL with pg connection string support. Keep credentials out of source code and use secret managers in production.
Do: Use environment variables for connection strings; enable SSL in production; use certificate-based auth when possible
Dont: Dont hardcode credentials in source code; dont disable SSL in production; dont commit .env files with database passwords

Description: Use named prepared statements for frequently executed queries. The pg library caches query plans for prepared statements, reducing parse overhead. Name statements consistently and keep them in a dedicated module for reuse.
Do: Use named prepared statements for hot-path queries; centralize statement definitions; benchmark with EXPLAIN
Dont: Dont use prepared statements for one-off queries; dont duplicate statement names; dont skip parameterization in prepared statements

Description: Use ioredis for Redis connections with automatic reconnection, Cluster support, and Sentinel support. Configure maxRetriesPerRequest, retryStrategy, and lazyConnect. Handle connection events for monitoring. Use a singleton instance across the application.
Do: Use ioredis with explicit retry and timeout configs; handle connect/error/close events; use lazyConnect in tests
Dont: Dont create multiple Redis instances unnecessarily; dont ignore connection error events; dont use the deprecated redis package for new projects

Description: Implement cache-aside (lazy-loading) pattern: check cache first, on miss fetch from database, then populate cache with a TTL. Use JSON serialization for complex objects. Invalidate cache on writes. Set appropriate TTL values to balance freshness and performance.
Do: Use cache-aside with TTL; invalidate on writes; serialize/deserialize consistently; use key prefixes
Dont: Dont cache without TTL; dont forget to invalidate on mutation; dont cache sensitive data without encryption

Description: Use ioredis Pub/Sub for broadcasting events across Node.js processes. Create a dedicated subscriber connection because a subscribed client cannot issue other commands. Parse message payloads safely and handle subscription errors.
Do: Use a dedicated Redis connection for subscriptions; validate message payloads; handle unsubscribe on shutdown
Dont: Dont reuse the subscriber connection for regular commands; dont send unparseable payloads; dont forget to unsubscribe on process exit

Description: Implement token bucket or sliding window rate limiting with Redis using INCR and EXPIRE or sorted sets. Use Lua scripts for atomic operations. Apply per-IP or per-user limits at the middleware level. Return proper 429 status codes with Retry-After headers.
Do: Use atomic Redis operations for rate limiting; implement sliding window for accuracy; return 429 with Retry-After
Dont: Dont implement rate limiting with in-memory counters in multi-process setups; dont use non-atomic read-then-write patterns

Description: Use connect-redis with express-session to store sessions in Redis for scalable, multi-process session management. Configure TTL, prefix, and serializer. Enable touch to refresh session expiry on activity. Use secure cookie settings in production.
Do: Use connect-redis with explicit TTL and prefix; enable secure cookies in production; use touch for session refresh
Dont: Dont store sessions in memory with MemoryStore in production; dont skip secure cookie flags; dont set excessive session TTL

Description: Use Redis Streams (XADD/XREAD/XREADGROUP) for durable event processing with consumer groups. Streams provide message persistence, consumer acknowledgment, and replay capability. Use consumer groups for parallel processing and XACK for reliable delivery.
Do: Use consumer groups for parallel processing; acknowledge messages with XACK; set MAXLEN to cap stream size
Dont: Dont use Pub/Sub when you need message durability; dont forget to XACK processed messages; dont let streams grow unbounded

Description: Define Mongoose schemas with strict typing, validation, and virtuals. Use TypeScript interfaces paired with schemas for type safety. Enable timestamps, set toJSON/toObject transforms, and use schema-level validation for data integrity.
Do: Define TypeScript interfaces alongside schemas; enable timestamps; add toJSON transforms to remove __v and rename _id
Dont: Dont use Schema.Types.Mixed without validation; dont skip required fields; dont define models without TypeScript interfaces

Description: Use Mongoose middleware (pre/post hooks) for cross-cutting concerns like password hashing, audit logging, and soft deletes. Use pre-save for data transformation and pre-find for query modification. Avoid heavy async operations in middleware that slow down queries.
Do: Use pre-save hooks for data transformation; use pre-find for automatic query filters; keep hooks lightweight
Dont: Dont put heavy I/O in middleware; dont forget to call next(); dont modify queries in post hooks when pre hooks suffice

Description: Use MongoDB aggregation pipelines for complex data transformations, grouping, and analytics. Place $match and $project stages early to reduce documents and fields processed. Use $lookup for joins. Type the pipeline result with TypeScript generics.
Do: Place $match stages first to leverage indexes; limit fields with $project early; type aggregation results
Dont: Dont load all documents into memory for aggregation in application code; dont skip $match before $group; dont use aggregation for simple find queries

Description: Create indexes on fields used in queries, sort operations, and unique constraints. Use compound indexes for multi-field queries matching left-prefix order. Use text indexes for search. Monitor with explain() and drop unused indexes.
Do: Use compound indexes matching query patterns; check with explain(); use TTL indexes for expiring data
Dont: Dont index every field; dont create redundant indexes; dont ignore index size impact on RAM

Description: Choose the MongoDB native driver for maximum performance and flexibility, or Mongoose for schema enforcement and developer productivity. Use Mongoose for CRUD-heavy apps with well-defined schemas. Use the native driver for high-throughput pipelines or when Mongoose overhead is measurable.
Do: Use Mongoose for schema validation and convenience; use native driver for performance-critical pipelines; evaluate both for your use case
Dont: Dont use Mongoose if you need raw performance and dont need schema validation; dont use native driver if your team benefits from Mongoose structure

Description: Implement health check endpoints that verify database connectivity. Check PostgreSQL with a simple SELECT 1, Redis with PING, and MongoDB with db.admin().ping(). Use timeouts to prevent health checks from hanging. Integrate with load balancers and orchestrators.
Do: Implement /health endpoints that verify each database connection; use timeouts; return structured health status
Dont: Dont skip database connectivity in health checks; dont let health checks hang without timeouts; dont expose connection details in health responses

Description: Create idempotent seed scripts that populate development and test databases with realistic data. Use upserts (ON CONFLICT) to avoid duplicates. Separate seed data from migration scripts. Support multiple environments (dev, test, staging).
Do: Use upsert patterns for idempotent seeding; separate seeds from migrations; tag seeds by environment
Dont: Dont use INSERT without conflict handling in seeds; dont seed production with test data; dont hardcode IDs that conflict with sequences

Description: Catch and classify database errors by code or type. PostgreSQL errors have a code field (23505 for unique violation, 23503 for foreign key). Map database errors to application-level errors with user-friendly messages. Never expose raw database errors to clients.
Do: Catch errors by database error code; map to application errors; log full error server-side; return safe messages to clients
Dont: Dont expose raw database errors to API clients; dont catch errors silently without logging; dont use generic catch-all error handling

Description: Log slow queries, failed queries, and query durations for observability. Use query middleware or wrapper functions to capture timing. Integrate with structured logging (pino, winston). Set slow-query thresholds and alert on them.
Do: Log query duration and text for slow queries; use structured logging; set alerting thresholds; redact sensitive parameters
Dont: Dont log query parameters containing PII or passwords; dont skip logging for failed queries; dont use console.log in production

Description: Automate database backups using pg_dump for PostgreSQL, Redis RDB/AOF snapshots, and mongodump for MongoDB. Schedule backups with cron or cloud-native tools. Test restore procedures regularly. Store backups offsite with encryption.
Do: Automate backups with scheduled jobs; test restore procedures; encrypt and store backups offsite; use point-in-time recovery for PostgreSQL
Dont: Dont rely on manual backups; dont store backups only on the database server; dont skip testing restore procedures

Description: Implement multi-tenancy with row-level isolation (tenant_id column), schema-level isolation (separate schemas per tenant), or database-level isolation. Row-level is simplest and most cost-effective. Always enforce tenant context in queries and middleware.
Do: Use row-level security with tenant_id columns; enforce tenant context in middleware; use PostgreSQL RLS policies
Dont: Dont rely on application code alone for tenant isolation; dont forget tenant_id in every query WHERE clause; dont mix tenant data without isolation
